sources.coverage_note: All 26 Insurance Core Principles (ICPs) with standards and guidance.

provisions.title: ICP 1 - Objectives, Powers and Responsibilities of the Supervisor
provisions.text: The authority (or authorities) responsible for insurance supervision and the objectives of insurance supervision are clearly defined. Legislation clearly defines the authority responsible for insurance supervision. The authority has adequate powers to perform its supervisory functions, including the ability to issue and enforce rules by administrative means, and adequate resources to fulfil its mandate. The objectives of insurance supervision include the protection of policyholders and contributing to the maintenance of financial stability.

provisions.title: ICP 2 - Supervisor
provisions.text: The supervisor, in the exercise of its functions and powers, is operationally independent, accountable and transparent; protects confidential information; has appropriate legal protection; has adequate financial resources; and meets high professional standards. The supervisor should be free from political interference and should have a stable source of funding that is not subject to undue influence.

provisions.title: ICP 3 - Information Exchange and Confidentiality
provisions.text: The supervisor exchanges information with other relevant supervisors and authorities subject to confidentiality, purpose and use requirements. Information exchange mechanisms should cover domestic and cross-border sharing. The supervisor should have the legal authority to share information and should have in place formal arrangements (such as MOUs) with other authorities.

provisions.title: ICP 4 - Licensing
provisions.text: A legal entity which intends to engage in insurance activities must be licensed before it can commence operations. The licensing requirements and procedures are clear, objective, and public. The licensing authority evaluates the fitness and propriety of significant owners, senior management, and key persons; the business plan; the capital adequacy; and the governance framework of the applicant.

provisions.title: ICP 5 - Suitability of Persons
provisions.text: The supervisor requires board members, senior management, key persons in control functions and significant owners of an insurer to be and remain suitable to fulfil their respective roles. Suitability requirements cover competence (qualifications, experience, knowledge) and integrity (honesty, reputation, absence of conflicts of interest). The supervisor has the power to remove individuals who are found to be unsuitable.

provisions.title: ICP 6 - Changes in Control and Portfolio Transfers
provisions.text: Supervisory approval is required for proposals to acquire significant ownership or controlling interest in an insurer and for portfolio transfers. The supervisor evaluates whether the proposed acquisition or transfer is in the interest of policyholders and the financial soundness of the insurer.

provisions.title: ICP 7 - Corporate Governance
provisions.text: The supervisor requires insurers to establish and implement a corporate governance framework which provides for sound and prudent management and oversight of the insurer's business and adequately recognises and protects the interests of policyholders. The framework should include: clear organisational structure with defined roles and responsibilities; effective risk management system; adequate internal control system; effective compliance function; and proper remuneration policies.

provisions.title: ICP 8 - Risk Management and Internal Controls
provisions.text: The supervisor requires an insurer to have, as part of its overall corporate governance framework, effective systems of risk management and internal controls, including effective functions for risk management, compliance, actuarial matters and internal audit. The risk management system should cover all material risks including insurance risk, market risk, credit risk, liquidity risk, operational risk, and concentration risk. The risk management function should be independent from risk-taking activities.

provisions.title: ICP 9 - Supervisory Review and Reporting
provisions.text: The supervisor takes a risk-based approach to supervision that uses both off-site monitoring and on-site inspections to examine the business of each insurer, evaluate its condition, the quality and effectiveness of its corporate governance and its compliance with relevant legislation and supervisory requirements. The supervisor requires insurers to submit information on a regular basis for both financial condition monitoring and market analysis purposes.

provisions.title: ICP 10 - Preventive and Corrective Measures
provisions.text: The supervisor takes preventive and corrective measures that are timely, suitable and necessary to achieve the objectives of insurance supervision. The supervisor has a range of preventive and corrective measures available, including: requiring an insurer to take specific actions; restricting business activities; directing an insurer to strengthen its financial position; replacing senior management; and appointing an administrator.

provisions.title: ICP 11 - Enforcement
provisions.text: The supervisor enforces corrective action in a timely manner where problems are identified and imposes sanctions where necessary. The supervisor has a range of enforcement tools including administrative penalties, public censure, licence revocation, and referral for criminal prosecution. Enforcement actions should be proportionate to the nature and severity of the non-compliance.

provisions.title: ICP 12 - Winding-up and Exit from the Market
provisions.text: The legislation defines a range of options for the exit of insurance legal entities from the market. It defines insolvency and establishes the criteria and procedure for dealing with an insurer that is failing or has failed. In the event of an insurer being wound up, the legal framework gives priority to the protection of policyholders. Policyholder claims should rank ahead of all other unsecured creditors.

provisions.title: ICP 13 - Reinsurance and Other Forms of Risk Transfer
provisions.text: The supervisor sets standards for the use of reinsurance and other forms of risk transfer, ensuring that insurers adequately control and transparently report their risk transfer programmes. The supervisor requires that: reinsurance arrangements are documented; counterparty risk is assessed and managed; concentration in reinsurance arrangements is monitored; and the insurer retains a meaningful amount of the risks it underwrites.

provisions.title: ICP 14 - Valuation
provisions.text: The supervisor establishes requirements for the valuation of assets and liabilities for solvency purposes. Valuation should be economic and based on consistent methodology. Technical provisions should be established for all insurance obligations and should be calculated based on a current estimate of the expected present value of all future cash flows arising from insurance contracts, plus a margin over the current estimate.

provisions.title: ICP 15 - Investment
provisions.text: The supervisor establishes requirements for solvency purposes on the investment activities of insurers in order to address the risks faced by insurers. Insurers should apply the prudent person principle: investments should be made in a manner appropriate to the nature and duration of the expected insurance liabilities; assets should be invested to ensure the security, quality, liquidity and profitability of the portfolio as a whole; and concentration risk should be managed.

provisions.title: ICP 16 - Enterprise Risk Management for Solvency Purposes
provisions.text: The supervisor requires the insurer to establish within its enterprise risk management framework an Own Risk and Solvency Assessment (ORSA). The ORSA should encompass all reasonably foreseeable and relevant material risks, including insurance, credit, market, operational and liquidity risks. The ORSA should determine the overall solvency needs of the insurer, considering the insurer's risk profile and risk appetite.

provisions.title: ICP 17 - Capital Adequacy
provisions.text: The supervisor establishes capital adequacy requirements for solvency purposes so that insurers can absorb significant unforeseen losses and to provide for degrees of supervisory intervention. The capital requirement should reflect the risk profile of the insurer. The capital requirement framework should include: a minimum capital requirement below which no insurer should operate; and a prescribed capital requirement (PCR) that establishes a solvency control level above which the supervisor does not intervene.

provisions.title: ICP 18 - Intermediaries
provisions.text: The supervisor sets and enforces requirements for the conduct of insurance intermediaries, to ensure that they conduct business in a professional and transparent manner. Requirements should cover: licensing or registration; competence and fitness; disclosure of status and remuneration; handling of client money; and complaints handling.

provisions.title: ICP 19 - Conduct of Business
provisions.text: The supervisor sets requirements for the conduct of the business of insurance to ensure customers are treated fairly, both before a contract is entered into and through to the point at which all obligations under a contract have been satisfied. Requirements should cover: product development and design; marketing and disclosure; advice and selling practices; after-sale service and claims handling; and complaints handling and dispute resolution.

provisions.title: ICP 20 - Public Disclosure
provisions.text: The supervisor requires insurers to disclose relevant, comprehensive and adequate information on a timely basis in order to give policyholders and market participants a clear view of their business activities, performance and financial position. This is expected to enhance market discipline and understanding of the risks to which an insurer is exposed and the manner in which those risks are managed.

provisions.title: ICP 21 - Countering Fraud in Insurance
provisions.text: The supervisor requires that insurers and intermediaries take effective measures to deter, prevent, detect, report and remedy fraud in insurance. Insurers should have anti-fraud policies and procedures, including fraud prevention programmes, fraud detection mechanisms, and reporting procedures for suspected fraud.

provisions.title: ICP 22 - Anti-Money Laundering and Combating the Financing of Terrorism
provisions.text: The supervisor requires insurers and intermediaries to take effective measures to combat money laundering and the financing of terrorism. At a minimum, insurers and intermediaries should be required to: develop programmes against money laundering and terrorist financing; report suspicious transactions; perform customer due diligence; and keep records. The AML/CFT requirements should be consistent with FATF Recommendations.

provisions.title: ICP 23 - Group-Wide Supervision
provisions.text: The supervisor supervises insurers on a legal entity and group-wide basis. Group-wide supervision should address: group structure and organisation; intra-group transactions; risk concentrations at group level; group governance and risk management; group capital adequacy; and the overall risk profile of the group. The group-wide supervisor should establish a supervisory college for internationally active insurance groups.

provisions.title: ICP 24 - Macroprudential Surveillance and Insurance Supervision
provisions.text: The supervisor identifies, monitors and analyses market and financial developments and other environmental factors that may impact insurers and insurance markets and uses this information in the supervision of individual insurers. The supervisor should have a macroprudential surveillance framework that allows it to: monitor trends in the insurance sector; identify emerging risks; assess the potential impact of macroeconomic developments on the insurance sector; and take appropriate action.

provisions.title: ICP 25 - Supervisory Cooperation and Coordination
provisions.text: The supervisor cooperates and coordinates with other relevant supervisors and authorities subject to confidentiality requirements. Cooperation and coordination should cover: information sharing; joint supervisory activities; crisis management and resolution; and policy development. The supervisor should establish and participate in supervisory colleges for internationally active insurance groups.

provisions.title: ICP 26 - Cross-Border Cooperation and Coordination on Crisis Management
provisions.text: The supervisor cooperates and coordinates with other relevant supervisors and authorities such that a cross-border crisis involving a specific insurer can be managed effectively. Crisis management frameworks should include: crisis management groups; institution-specific cross-border cooperation agreements; information sharing arrangements; and coordinated resolution plans.

definitions.definition: An assessment conducted by the insurer of the adequacy of its risk management and current and likely future solvency position, taking into account the insurer's specific risk profile and risk appetite.

A capital requirement that establishes a solvency control level above which the supervisor does not intervene on capital adequacy grounds. The PCR should be established at a level that provides a higher degree of protection for policyholders than the minimum capital requirement.
